full_response:
Let the bag–weights be the distinct positive numbers  

  w₁ , w₂ , … , wₙ , where the indices are arranged so that  

  w₁ > w₂ > ··· > wₙ .                                                   (1)

Greedy algorithm  
Greedy repeatedly takes the heaviest remaining bag until the running total is at least W:

Greedy‐Pick                                                               
 total ← 0,   k ← 0                                                       
 while total < W do                                                       
     k ← k+1                                                              
     take bag k (weight w_k)                                              
     total ← total + w_k                                                  
 return the k chosen bags

Let  

  G = { w₁ , w₂ , … , w_k }                                              (2)

where k is the first index for which ∑_{i=1}^k w_i ≥ W.

Goal  
Show that no feasible solution can use fewer than k bags; hence Greedy is optimal (uses the minimum possible number of bags).

=====================================================================
Key lemma
For each ℓ = 1,2,…,n, the ℓ heaviest bags have weight at least as large as any other ℓ bags:

  For every subset S with |S| = ℓ,  ∑_{i=1}^{ℓ} w_i ≥ ∑_{b∈S} b.     (3)

Proof.  
Because (1) orders the weights in non-increasing order, the largest element of S is ≤ w₁, the second largest ≤ w₂, …, the ℓ-th largest ≤ w_ℓ.  Summing these ℓ pair-wise inequalities gives (3). ∎

=====================================================================
Optimality proof by contradiction
Assume there exists a feasible solution T that uses m bags with m < k; i.e.

  |T| = m,        and        ∑_{b∈T} b ≥ W.                           (4)

Because |T| = m, inequality (3) (with ℓ = m) implies

  ∑_{i=1}^{m} w_i ≥ ∑_{b∈T} b ≥ W.                                   (5)

But k was defined to be the smallest integer such that the first k bags reach W.  
Inequality (5) says the first m bags already reach W, contradicting the minimality of k because m < k.

Hence no solution can use strictly fewer than k bags; Greedy’s solution uses the minimum possible number of bags.

=====================================================================
Complexity
• If we first sort the n weights, Greedy uses O(n log n) time and O(1) extra space.  
• With a linear-time selection algorithm to find the k heaviest prefix, the running time can be reduced to O(n).

=====================================================================
Summary
The earliest prefix of the weights list whose sum reaches W is always at least as heavy as any other collection of the same cardinality; therefore the size k of that prefix is a lower bound on the number of bags required, and Greedy achieves that bound.

\boxed{\text{Repeatedly taking the heaviest remaining bag yields an optimal (minimum–cardinality) set of bags whose total weight is }\ge W.}

Confidence: 93%
extracted_answer: \text{Repeatedly taking the heaviest remaining bag yields an optimal (minimum–cardinality) set of bags whose total weight is }\ge W.
